title: Rejects invocation when required flag value is missing
given: Given a target directory exists and the CLI is invoked with --dir /tmp/work --find old --replace but no value following --replace
then: Then the CLI exits with a non-zero status and prints a clear usage or validation error instead of attempting any rename work

title: Treats special-character pattern and replacement as literal filename text
given: Given a directory contains files named report[a].txt and report$.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find [a] --replace Ω and then separately with --find $ --replace _end
then: Then only filenames containing the exact literal text [a] or $ are renamed, and files not containing those literal characters are unchanged

title: Accepts empty replacement string for removal renames
given: Given a directory contains files named old_notes.txt and keep.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old_ --replace ""
then: Then the tool renames old_notes.txt to notes.txt and leaves keep.txt unchanged rather than rejecting the empty replacement as missing input

title: Recursively renames matching files in nested directories only
given: Given a target directory contains root_old.txt, a/b/c/deep_old.txt, and a directory named archive_old with a file inside named untouched.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace new
then: Then root_old.txt and a/b/c/deep_old.txt are renamed, but the directory archive_old itself is not renamed and untouched.txt remains unchanged because only file names are in scope

title: Handles Unicode filenames during recursive traversal
given: Given a target directory contains nested files named café_old.txt, emoji_📁_old.md, and RTL_שלום_old.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace new
then: Then each matching file is renamed successfully with the non-ASCII characters preserved exactly in the resulting filenames

title: Replaces every occurrence of the find string within a filename
given: Given a directory contains files named old_old_old.txt and oldkeep.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace new
then: Then old_old_old.txt becomes new_new_new.txt and oldkeep.txt becomes newkeep.txt, showing all occurrences in each filename are replaced

title: Does not overwrite when two source filenames map to the same destination
given: Given a directory contains files named a_old.txt and a_old_old.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find _old --replace ""
then: Then the CLI reports a name collision for the conflicting target filename, does not silently overwrite either file, and leaves the filesystem in a safe, observable state

title: Dry-run previews recursive changes without modifying any files
given: Given a target directory contains nested matching files and their original names are recorded before execution
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace new --dry-run
then: Then the CLI lists each planned rename and after the command completes every file still exists under its original name with no renamed files created

title: Dry-run reports collisions and permission problems as previews without partial mutation
given: Given a target directory contains one rename that would collide with an existing filename and another matching file in a subdirectory without rename permission
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace "" --dry-run
then: Then the output identifies both the planned rename and the blocking issues, and no file or directory contents are changed despite the mixed outcomes

title: Reports no matches found cleanly and exits without error
given: Given a readable target directory tree contains no filenames with the requested find string
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find missing --replace new
then: Then the CLI reports that no matching files were found, makes no filesystem changes, and exits successfully

title: Continues or fails predictably on permission-denied files without hiding the error
given: Given a target directory contains two matching files where one is renameable and one is in a subdirectory lacking permission for rename
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace new
then: Then the CLI surfaces the permission error clearly and its final output and exit status reflect the partial failure rather than reporting full success

title: Prevents path-like replacement text from escaping the original directory
given: Given a target directory contains a file named note_old.txt
when: When the CLI is run with --dir <dir> --find old --replace ../escaped
then: Then the tool does not move or rename the file outside the target directory, and instead rejects or safely handles the replacement text as invalid filename content on the platform
